claimed_contribution: The paper introduces and documents 'Harbingers of failure': customers whose early adoption of new products is a negative signal of long-run product success.
It proposes two practical ways to identify Harbingers from transaction data: prior purchases of failed new products (FlopAffinity) and prior purchases of niche existing products.
It shows how separating early sales by customer type can be incorporated into new product decisions and materially improve prediction versus using aggregate early sales alone.
context: The study is set in a large U.S. convenience-store chain selling consumer packaged goods (beauty, consumer healthcare, edibles, and general merchandise). The data come from 111 stores across 14 Midwestern and Southwestern states.
design_and_data: The authors combine weekly store-level item transactions (Jan 2003-Oct 2009) with individual loyalty-card histories (over 10 million transactions, 127,925 sampled customers, Nov 2003-Nov 2005). They study 8,809 new products and define success primarily as surviving at least 3 years after introduction. Customers are first grouped using a classification set of new products based on each customer's FlopAffinity (share of purchased new products that later failed), then product success in a later prediction set is modeled from first-15-week sales using binary logit models that either aggregate all sales or separate sales by customer groups. They also test repeat-purchase-based grouping, alternative success metrics (2-year survival, market share, hazard), alternative sample constructions, holdout prediction, and subgroup analyses.
main_findings: Early sales are not uniformly good news: in product-level logits, sales from high-FlopAffinity customers (especially Group 4) are negatively associated with 3-year survival, while aggregate sales alone are positively associated with success.
The failure signal strengthens when Harbingers repeatedly buy the new item; coefficients for high-FlopAffinity groups become more negative as the minimum repeat threshold rises.

Customers with high FlopAffinity disproportionately buy niche and very niche existing products, and niche-based identification predicts failure similarly to FlopAffinity; combining both signals improves predictive performance further.
one_line_summary: Using convenience-store transaction data, the paper shows that early purchases by certain customers (Harbingers) predict new product failure rather than success and improve forecasting when modeled separately.
relation_to_prior_work: challenges the standard new-product forecasting premise that stronger early sales are uniformly positive by showing purchaser composition can reverse the signal. It complements lead-user work by adding that some early adopters are informative in the opposite direction. It also links to preference-minority research by showing that failure-predictive customers are more likely to buy niche existing items.
research_gap: Prior work and practice largely use the level of early sales as a positive indicator of eventual success and pay limited attention to which customers generated those sales. The paper addresses the unresolved possibility that a systematic subset of early adopters may be unrepresentative and therefore signal future failure.
research_question: Do purchases by different early adopters carry different information about whether a newly introduced product will succeed or fail? Specifically, can firms identify customers whose adoption predicts failure, and can those customers be detected both from past failed new-product purchases and from purchases of niche existing products?
